pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Automate drafting, summarization, and artifact linking as default behaviors when outputs are clearly marked as AI-generated and intended for inspection before action.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly approves automatic drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when clearly marked as generated and notes humans can inspect before action. S5 lists drafting and artifact linking as safe defaults, and S4 supports internal draft summaries as advisory if not confused with commitments.

claimJudgments.claimText: Require human approval (with an accountable approver recorded) for policy changes, customer commitments, release blocking, and external/customer-facing messages because they change obligations or can stop work.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 rejects automatic policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking because they change obligations or stop work. S5 says release blocks, customer commitments, policy changes, and external messages require an approver recorded in the artifact trail. S4 requires an accountable human reviewer for customer-facing commitments.

claimJudgments.claimText: Add explicit status labels (e.g., draft, recommendation, approved action, blocked) and ensure recommendations cannot be presented in a way that looks like an approved decision, to prevent unnecessary pauses or mistaken execution.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 describes an incident where a recommendation was mistaken for an approved decision and teams paused work, and asks for the exact status labels listed. S6 also recommends visible status labels and distinguishing generated content from approved action.

claimJudgments.claimText: Design the interface to visually separate evidence, recommendations, and action controls (avoiding a combined recommendation-and-execute cluster) to improve acceptance and reduce cancellations/clarification behavior.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly reports higher acceptance when evidence, recommendation, and action controls are separated, and more cancellations/clarification clicks when recommendation and action are combined. S6 reinforces separating panels and avoiding an execution button beside an AI recommendation.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation directly answers which actions should be automated versus require approval, and its approval boundaries and UI safeguards are well supported by the packet. It does not materially overstate the evidence.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S5 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
